items.question: The Single Responsibility Principle is best stated as:
options: A class should have only one method
A class should have one reason to change
Each class needs its own file
items.explanation: SRP is about axes of change (stakeholders), not size or method count.
items.relatedLessonTitle: SOLID I: Single Responsibility Principle

items.question: The main benefit of constructor dependency injection is:
options: Dependencies are visible, swappable, and fakeable in tests
items.explanation: Explicit dependencies in the constructor make coupling visible and let tests inject fakes — the practical payoff of DIP.
items.relatedLessonTitle: SOLID V: Dependency Inversion Principle

items.question: Passing an IComparer<T> to List<T>.Sort is an example of which pattern?
items.explanation: Sort owns the invariant algorithm; the injected comparer is the swappable piece — Strategy.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Strategy: Swappable Algorithms

items.question: In modern ASP.NET Core, the recommended way to get a single shared instance of a service is:
options: services.AddSingleton<IFoo, Foo>() — singleton the lifetime, not the pattern
items.explanation: Container-managed lifetime keeps the single instance injectable, visible, and replaceable in tests.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Singleton: The Pattern You Should Rarely Hand-Roll

items.question: The highest-value Builder you'll typically write yourself is:
options: A fluent test-data builder with sensible defaults
items.explanation: Tests state only what they care about; defaults cover the rest — kills the 9-parameter constructor repeated in 40 tests.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Builder: Constructing Complex Objects Step by Step

items.question: Your business code calls a third-party SDK's types directly in controllers. The pattern that fixes this is:
options: Adapter — wrap the SDK behind an interface your domain owns
items.explanation: Vendor types stop at the adapter boundary; swapping vendors becomes one new adapter instead of surgery.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Adapter: Making Incompatible Interfaces Fit

items.question: Facade differs from Adapter in that a Facade:
options: Converts one interface to match another
Provides one simplified entry point over a whole subsystem
items.explanation: Adapter = shape conversion for compatibility; Facade = convenience front door over multiple components.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Facade: One Simple Door to a Complex Subsystem

items.question: A switch statement over shipping carriers keeps growing with each new carrier. The idiomatic ASP.NET Core fix is:
options: Move the switch to a helper class
Strategy classes registered in DI, injected as IEnumerable<T>, resolved by matching
items.explanation: New carrier = new class + one registration; zero modification of tested code — OCP via the container.
items.relatedLessonTitle: SOLID II: Open/Closed Principle

items.question: A singleton service takes a scoped DbContext in its constructor. What happens?
options: Captive dependency: one context frozen for the app's lifetime — concurrency chaos; ASP.NET Core throws in Development
items.explanation: The singleton outlives the scope and captures the context forever. Inject IDbContextFactory or create scopes manually instead.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Singleton: The Pattern You Should Rarely Hand-Roll

items.question: Decorator beats subclassing for cross-cutting concerns (caching, logging) because:
options: Layers compose at runtime in any order and apply to any implementation of the interface
items.explanation: Inheritance fixes behavior at compile time to one parent; decorators wrap any implementation and stack freely.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Decorator: Layering Behavior Transparently

items.question: Placing UseAuthorization before UseAuthentication in the pipeline breaks because:
options: Chain order defines data flow: authorization needs the identity that authentication attaches to the context
items.explanation: ASP.NET Core middleware is Chain of Responsibility — each link consumes what earlier links produced.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Chain of Responsibility: The Middleware Pattern

items.question: C# event subscriptions cause memory leaks when:
options: A long-lived publisher holds strong references to short-lived subscribers that never unsubscribe
items.explanation: The publisher's delegate list keeps subscribers reachable — they can't be garbage collected until -= or publisher death.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Observer: Publish/Subscribe in Process

items.question: A BackgroundService must react to appsettings.json changes at runtime. Inject:
items.explanation: IOptions is frozen at first use; IOptionsSnapshot is scoped (throws in a singleton); IOptionsMonitor gives live values + OnChange.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Options Pattern: Configuration Done Right

items.question: A subclass overrides an interface method to throw NotSupportedException. The cleanest fix is usually:
options: Split the interface so the type only implements what it truly supports
items.explanation: The hierarchy claims a capability the type lacks (LSP violation); narrowing the contract (ISP) fixes the lie.
items.relatedLessonTitle: SOLID III: Liskov Substitution Principle

items.question: A generic Repository<T> exposing IQueryable<T> Query() fails as an abstraction because:
options: Callers still compose provider-specific queries — the persistence details it claimed to hide leak through
It can't be registered in DI
items.explanation: Swap EF for Dapper and every IQueryable call site breaks — the boundary is fictional. Domain-specific repositories with named methods are the real pattern.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Repository & Unit of Work: Use, Misuse, and EF Core

items.question: The cheapest genuinely valuable form of CQRS in a typical ASP.NET Core app is:
options: Separate command/query handlers over one database — queries use AsNoTracking projections to DTOs
items.explanation: Level-1 CQRS: cleaner code and measurably faster reads with zero consistency or infrastructure cost.
items.relatedLessonTitle: CQRS: Separate Read and Write Models

items.question: The circuit breaker's Open state exists to:
options: Fail fast without calling the dependency — protecting it from load and you from thread/socket exhaustion
items.explanation: Continuing to call a dying dependency wastes your resources and delays its recovery; instant failure contains the cascade.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Resilience Patterns: Retry, Circuit Breaker, Timeout

items.question: Hangfire's BackgroundJob.Enqueue(() => Send(id)) embodies the Command pattern because:
options: It runs on a thread pool
The invocation is captured as serializable data — storable, delayable, retryable, executable elsewhere
items.explanation: Turning a method call into inspectable, persistable data is Command's essence — enabling queues, retries, and audit.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Command: Requests as Objects

items.question: You have an Order entity where every method starts with if/switch on Status, and statuses keep growing. Full State pattern beats a transition table when:
options: States have rich, differing behavior — not just allowed/forbidden transitions
items.explanation: A Dictionary<(Status,Trigger),Status> handles pure transition rules; state classes earn their weight when each state's logic is substantial.
items.relatedLessonTitle: State: Behavior That Changes with Status

items.question: Visitor is the right choice when:
options: Types change often but operations are fixed
The type hierarchy is stable and you keep adding new operations over it (e.g., EF's ExpressionVisitor)
items.explanation: Visitor trades easy-to-add-operations for hard-to-add-types. EF Core translating your lambdas to SQL walks a fixed Expression node set — the canonical fit.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Memento & Visitor: Snapshots and Double Dispatch

items.question: A colleague proposes interfaces + factories for a stable class with one implementation, 'just in case'. The senior response is:
options: Rule of three: add the abstraction when change pressure is demonstrated, not speculatively
items.explanation: Patterns pay for indirection with flexibility; flexibility for changes that never come is pure cost.
items.relatedLessonTitle: Pattern Selection: Smells → Patterns Cheat Sheet
